title: Delete 12 dead has*TokensForAgent/Board exports (zero call sites repo-wide)
description: Every provider file exports a hasXTokensForAgent + hasXTokensForBoard pair: gdrive.ts:32-40, gmail.ts:31-39, onedrive.ts:28-36, outlook.ts:33-41, github.ts:128-136, slack.ts:90-98. A repo-wide grep (including frontend and tests, excluding node_modules) finds zero importers — the only matches are the 12 definitions themselves. They are 1-line wrappers around hasOAuthToken and mislead readers into thinking external callers depend on these files' token-presence semantics. In github.ts and slack.ts the hasOAuthToken import (github.ts:4, slack.ts:4) exists solely to feed these dead functions, so it goes too.
sketch: Delete the 12 functions. In github.ts and slack.ts also drop hasOAuthToken from the database.js import list (gdrive/gmail/onedrive/outlook keep it — their /status handlers use it). Verify with tsc + grep that nothing references them.
impact: ~54 lines removed across 6 files, 2 unused imports removed; shrinks each provider file's apparent public API to what is actually consumed (the get*AccessTokenForAgent functions and the router factory)
guidance: Sketch is correct as written; extend it to 6 identical dead siblings the proposal missed: hasJiraCredentialsForAgent/Board (api/src/routes/jira.ts:19-27), hasS3CredentialsForAgent/Board (api/src/routes/s3.ts:14-22), hasWordPressCredentialsForAgent/Board (api/src/routes/wordpress.ts:39-47) — same grep methodology shows zero call sites. In all three of those files hasOAuthToken is used only by these dead wrappers (jira.ts:3/21/26, s3.ts:4/16/21, wordpress.ts:3/41/46), so drop that import there too, bringing the total to 18 dead functions and 5 unused imports. No behavior-preservation traps: nothing references any of these symbols, the wrappers have no side effects, and api tsc will catch any missed reference.

title: Single resolveScope: one function copy-pasted 8 times
description: The byte-identical 5-line agent→board→user scope resolver appears 8 times: gdrive.ts:26-30, gmail.ts:25-29, onedrive.ts:18-22, outlook.ts:23-27, github.ts:73-77, slack.ts:76-80, googleOAuth.ts:103-111, microsoftOAuth.ts:107-115. This is THE core token-scoping rule of the whole plugin system (documented in 4 separate file-header comments), yet a change to it would require 8 synchronized edits. Six of the copies are even untyped (implicit any params) while the two callback files have the typed version.
sketch: export function resolveScope(agentId: string | null, boardId: string | null, username: string | undefined): { scopeType: ScopeType; scopeId: string } in api/src/routes/oauthHelper.ts (it already is the shared OAuth utility module). Replace all 8 local definitions with the import. Pure move — identical body, the typed variant.
impact: ~45 lines removed; the scope-resolution rule gets exactly one definition next to its documentation; trivially safe first step that the larger proposals below build on
guidance: 1) Not a literal 'pure move': with the typed signature, tsc (gate) breaks at the 6 /status call sites that pass uncast query values — gmail.ts:95-96→99, gdrive.ts:96-97→100, onedrive.ts:96-97→100, outlook.ts:97-98→101, github.ts:263-264→267, slack.ts:182-183→186 — because Express types req.query.X as string|string[]|ParsedQs|ParsedQs[], not assignable to string|null even under strict:false. Add the cast pattern already used in the same files' /auth-url handlers: `(req.query.agentId as string | undefined) || null` (cf. gmail.ts:120-121). Casts are compile-time only; runtime identical (including the existing quirk that array-valued query params flow through as truthy scopeIds — do not 'fix' that here). Disconnect handlers use req.body (any) and the two callback files pass typed stateData — no casts needed there. 2) Do NOT absorb wordpress.ts:26, s3.ts:8, jira.ts:13 — that is a different 2-param resolveScope returning `| null` with no user fallback; merging it would change behavior. 3) In oauthHelper.ts use `import type { ScopeType } from '../services/database.js'` to keep the module runtime-dependency-free. 4) username typed `string | undefined` vs the callbacks' `string` is equivalent under strict:false; `string | undefined` is the more honest choice since gmail.ts:97 et al. pass `req.user?.username`.

title: createOAuthStateStore factory: 4 copies of HMAC-signed state machinery (~250 lines of security-critical clone)
description: The full stateless-state implementation — STATE_TTL_MS, consumedStates Map, fallbackStateSecret, getStateSecret (HKDF from JWT_SECRET), signStatePayload, generateOAuthState, consumeOAuthState — is copy-pasted 4 times: googleOAuth.ts:32-101, microsoftOAuth.ts:34-105, github.ts:15-71, slack.ts:15-74. The only real differences are the HKDF domain string ('pulsarteam:oauth-state:google:v1' etc.) and the payload shape (google adds `service`, microsoft adds `service`+`consumerFlow`). microsoftOAuth.ts:143-151 additionally has a `peekServiceFromState` that re-parses the payload by hand. This is timing-safe-compare + replay-guard crypto code: any future fix (e.g. the consumed-states sweep, TTL handling) must currently be applied 4 times, and the copies have already drifted cosmetically (github.ts:45 compresses the sweep to one line; slack.ts builds `entry` separately). Only googleOAuth's copy has tests.
impact: ~250 duplicated lines collapse to ~85 shared + 4 one-line instantiations (net ~-160); the replay/TTL/signature logic gets one audited, tested home instead of four drifting copies
guidance: Corrections and traps for the implementer: (1) Keep BOTH consumedStates Map and fallbackStateSecret per-factory-instance (closure-scoped), never module-level shared — the dev fallback path (no JWT_SECRET) has no domain separation, so a shared fallback secret would let a google-issued state verify under another provider's consume, a behavior change. (2) The sketched generic consume(state): T | null glosses over per-provider normalization: google/github/slack return agentId: entry.agentId || null, boardId: entry.boardId || null; microsoft additionally coerces consumerFlow: !!entry.consumerFlow. Do not return the raw JSON.parse result typed as T (it carries nonce/expiresAt and skips the || null coercion); either have the factory take a normalize/project callback or keep the field-mapping in the thin per-provider wrappers. (3) peek must NOT verify the signature — microsoftOAuth.ts:143-151 parses the payload unverified by design (try/catch swallow) so error popups can still be routed; keep the 'onedrive'|'outlook' value whitelist in the microsoft wrapper or call site, not the generic factory. (4) The sketch says to 'port' googleOAuthState.test.ts — unnecessary: with generateGoogleOAuthState/consumeGoogleOAuthState kept as exported wrappers the test file runs unchanged; leave it untouched and optionally add factory-level tests. (5) github.ts:43/53 and slack.ts:43/56 have implicit-any params; call sites github.ts:288 and slack.ts:214 pass req.query.agentId || null (Express qs types). A typed generate(entry: T) will surface tsc errors there — fix with type assertions (as string | null), not runtime coercions like String(), which would change embedded values for array-shaped query params. (6) Preserve sweep-before-insert ordering (sweep runs in generate only, not consume) and key the replay map on the full state string. (7) As the sketch already flags: the four HKDF domain strings must stay byte-identical ('pulsarteam:oauth-state:{google|microsoft|github|slack}:v1') so states issued before a rolling deploy still verify after it when JWT_SECRET is set.

title: Shared oauthProviderRoutes + refreshProviderToken: gdrive/gmail/onedrive/outlook are ~85% the same file
description:
Quantified absorption for the suspected clone family (the four files total 640 lines):
(1) Token refresh — refreshGdriveToken (gdrive.ts:42-85), refreshGmailToken (gmail.ts:41-84), refreshOnedriveToken (onedrive.ts:38-85), refreshOutlookToken (outlook.ts:43-86) are ~180 lines differing ONLY in provider string, log label, config getter, and token-endpoint URL (onedrive adds the consumerFlow tenant pick at onedrive.ts:53). The subtle invalid_grant-vs-transient deletion policy (the comment at gdrive.ts:63-64 etc.) is maintained in quadruplicate.
(2) GET /status — gdrive.ts:94-113, gmail.ts:93-112, onedrive.ts:94-112, outlook.ts:95-114, plus github.ts:261-278 and slack.ts:180-197: six ~19-line handlers identical except the identity field name (email/login/teamName/username) and github/slack's connected predicate.
(3) POST /disconnect — gdrive.ts:145-153, gmail.ts:147-155, onedrive.ts:154-162, outlook.ts:150-158, github.ts:300-308, slack.ts:226-234: six 9-line handlers identical except the provider string and log label.
(4) GET /auth-url — same scaffold (config guard, agentId/boardId extraction, state, redirect URI, URLSearchParams, res.json) in all six; only scopes/endpoint/extra params differ.
Also: onedrive.ts:24-26 and outlook.ts:29-31 define getConfig() as a pure no-op alias of getMicrosoftOAuthConfig, while github.ts:79-84 and slack.ts:82-88 give the SAME name to a real env-reading config builder — confusing when reading files side by side; the spec object removes the aliases.
NOT absorbable (stays per-provider): onedrive's consumerFlow login_hint block (onedrive.ts:122-149), github's fetchWithRetry + redirect router, slack's comma-joined scopes and fixed env redirectUri, the four getXAccessTokenForAgent exports (consumed by the *Mcp.ts services).
impact: Net ~-320 lines across 6 files (640 → ~300 for the four clones, plus ~55 from github/slack handlers); the refresh deletion policy, status semantics, and disconnect logic each get exactly one implementation; adding a future provider becomes a ~35-line spec instead of a 160-line copy-paste
guidance: Sketch corrections (all minor, none invalidating): (1) The spec needs TWO not-configured messages, not one: the refresh functions throw short messages ('Google Drive not configured' gdrive.ts:44, 'Gmail not configured' gmail.ts:43, 'OneDrive not configured' onedrive.ts:40, 'Outlook not configured' outlook.ts:45) while the /auth-url 500s return longer setup-instruction messages that ALSO differ between gdrive (gdrive.ts:118, long variant mentioning client reuse) and gmail (gmail.ts:117, short variant) despite sharing a config getter. Do not derive either from the log label ('Gdrive' ≠ 'Google Drive'). (2) Make the spec generic over the config type (OAuthProviderSpec<TConfig>): slack's getConfig returns {clientId, clientSecret, redirectUri} (slack.ts:82-88) and its buildAuthUrl needs config.redirectUri — the sketched {clientId; clientSecret; tenantId?} type would drop it. (3) Preserve the non-equivalent connected predicates as explicit overrides: default hasOAuthToken for the four refresh providers (expired-but-refreshable counts as connected, comment gdrive.ts:102-103), github !!(token && token.accessToken) (github.ts:269), slack !!token (slack.ts:188) — these three are NOT interchangeable; do not harmonize. (4) onedrive parses consumerFlow from req.query for both state generation (onedrive.ts:122,129 — extra 5th arg to generateMicrosoftOAuthState) and URL building (135,146-149 — tenant pick + login_hint + prompt); with generateState(...,req) and buildAuthUrl(req,...) it gets parsed twice, which is fine (pure), but both must read consumer==='1'||'true' identically. (5) onedrive's /status identity field is the requesting username (onedrive.ts:108), not token meta — keep statusFields(token, connected, username) as sketched. (6) Keep thrown-error composition verbatim: data.error_description || data.error || `Token refresh failed (HTTP ${status})`, and the exact warn/log line formats incl. emoji prefixes and scopeType:scopeId. (7) resolveScope is identically duplicated in all six files — move it into the shared module too (pure, zero risk); the sketch implies but doesn't state this. (8) Bonus dead code: all twelve hasXTokensForAgent/Board exports (gdrive.ts:32-40, gmail.ts:31-39, onedrive.ts:28-36, outlook.ts:33-41, github.ts:128-136, slack.ts:90-98) are imported nowhere — delete them in the same pass rather than porting them into specs. (9) No tests cover these handlers; land as two commits (four-clone family first, then github/slack adopting oauthProviderRoutes) to keep diffs reviewable, and rely on tsc + manual diff of each provider's emitted URLs/JSON against the originals. (10) Keep onedrive's oddly-named getAccessTokenForAgent export name as-is (onedriveMcp.ts:4 imports it) — renaming is out of scope for behavior preservation.

title: Rename onedrive's generically-named getAccessTokenForAgent
description: onedrive.ts:87 exports getAccessTokenForAgent — the only one of six siblings without a provider prefix (getGdriveAccessTokenForAgent, getGmailAccessTokenForAgent, getOutlookAccessTokenForAgent, getGitHubAccessTokenForAgent, getSlackAccessTokenForAgent). At its call sites in onedriveMcp.ts:23, 273, 306 the bare name reads like a generic multi-provider resolver rather than a OneDrive-specific token fetch, and it shadows the mental slot occupied by database.js's resolveAccessToken.
sketch: Rename to getOnedriveAccessTokenForAgent in onedrive.ts:87 and update the import + 3 call sites in onedriveMcp.ts (lines 4, 23, 273, 306). Pure rename, internal consumers only.
impact: 5 edit sites; eliminates the one misleadingly-generic name in an otherwise uniform six-provider naming scheme
guidance: Sketch is accurate as-is. One precision: the function is async, so the renamed declaration is `export async function getOnedriveAccessTokenForAgent(agentId, boardId = null)`. Adjacent observation for the parent survey (do not fold into this rename): all six get*AccessTokenForAgent functions are identical one-line wrappers around resolveAccessToken('<provider>', agentId, boardId, refresh<Provider>Token) — a separate duplication proposal could collapse them, and doing the rename now neither blocks nor is blocked by that.

title: Move hardcoded personal email login_hint out of onedrive auth-url code
description: onedrive.ts:147 hardcodes params.set('login_hint', 'gvinsot@hotmail.com') — the owner's personal email baked into shared route logic, with a 4-line comment (onedrive.ts:131-134) explaining it pins a specific B2B-guest identity. Any other user clicking the consumer-flow connect gets someone else's email pre-filled. As code, it makes a general-purpose route read like a one-user patch and is the single most surprising line in the nine-file scope.
sketch: const consumerLoginHint = process.env.MICROSOFT_CONSUMER_LOGIN_HINT || 'gvinsot@hotmail.com'; (default preserves today's behavior exactly) and params.set('login_hint', consumerLoginHint). Keep the explanatory comment, now describing the env knob. Optionally drop the hint entirely when the env var is unset in a later behavior-reviewed change.
impact: 1 magic value becomes configuration; the consumer-flow branch reads as a feature instead of a personal workaround
guidance: Read process.env inside the request handler (or extend getMicrosoftOAuthConfig) rather than at module top-level — the existing pattern in api/src/services/microsoftOAuthConfig.ts:28-35 reads env per call. Document MICROSOFT_CONSUMER_LOGIN_HINT in the env-var comment block at microsoftOAuthConfig.ts:11-14, the established home for Microsoft env vars. Note '|| default' means an empty env var cannot disable the hint (falls back to the default); keep it that way for this change and leave hint removal to the later behavior-reviewed step the sketch already proposes. Do not touch the adjacent params.set('prompt', 'select_account') at onedrive.ts:148.

title: Unify the Google/Microsoft OAuth callback handlers behind one token-exchange engine
description: handleGoogleOAuthCallback (googleOAuth.ts:158-229) and handleMicrosoftOAuthCallback (microsoftOAuth.ts:153-232) are step-for-step the same ~75-line flow: provider-error guard → code/state guard → consume state → config guard → form-urlencoded code exchange with 15s timeout → fetchUserEmail (googleOAuth.ts:113-127 vs microsoftOAuth.ts:117-131, same shape with different URL/fields) → resolveScope → storeOAuthToken with throwOnPersistError → result page; even the comments match ('Must match the redirect_uri sent in the auth URL — derive the same way.'). Real divergences are small and enumerable: token endpoint (fixed Google URL vs tenant/consumerFlow-dependent), profile endpoint/field, meta payload, and the postMessage strategy (Google: per-service message types via googleOAuthResult/MESSAGE_TYPE_BY_SERVICE at lines 129-156; Microsoft: single 'microsoft-oauth-callback' type + service field + peekServiceFromState). Today a fix to the exchange flow (e.g. the timeout, error mapping, persist semantics) must be mirrored by hand.
impact: ~150 lines of mirrored flow collapse to ~75 shared + 2 hook objects (net ~-50, but the bigger win is that exchange-flow fixes stop needing manual mirroring); pairs naturally with the state-store factory proposal
guidance:
Traps to respect (all verified against the code):

1. sendResult as sketched cannot reproduce Microsoft's early-error behavior. Microsoft calls peekServiceFromState(rawState) on the provider-error branch (microsoftOAuth.ts:158) and the missing-code/state branch (:163), but deliberately NOT on the invalid/expired-state branch (:168) — that message carries no service field, so no widget claims it and the error shows only in the popup (OneDriveConnect.tsx:53 filters on event.data.service). Since 'missing code' can occur with a present raw state, (consumedState, rawState) alone cannot distinguish the peek branch from the no-peek branch. Pass the raw state string AND a stage discriminator to the error-send hook (e.g. sendError(res, stage: 'provider_error'|'missing_params'|'bad_state'|'not_configured'|'exchange_failed'|'internal', consumed: S|null, rawState: string|undefined, error: string)), or keep separate early-error hooks. Google's early branches map to service=null (gmail-oauth-callback fallback, googleOAuth.ts:148-151) — preserve that too.

2. fetchProfileEmail(accessToken) as sketched loses Google's per-service warn label: googleOAuth.ts:113-127 logs `[gmail]`/`[gdrive]` while Microsoft logs `[Microsoft]` (microsoftOAuth.ts:128). Pass the consumed state into the hook: fetchProfileEmail(accessToken, state).

3. Console log labels differ throughout: Google logs `[${stateData.service}]` ('gmail'/'gdrive'), Microsoft logs `[${providerLabel}]` ('OneDrive'/'Outlook'). Add a logLabel(state) hook if you want strict log parity (logs are not wire format, but operators may grep them).

4. provider passed to storeOAuthToken is stateData.service in both — make the generic S carry service, or add a provider(state) hook; do not hardcode.

5. Do NOT pull github.ts (handleOAuthRedirect, api/src/routes/github.ts:179-250) or slack.ts (:105-169) into the same engine in this pass, even though they share the skeleton: their guard ORDER differs (config check before state consume — github.ts:192-200, slack.ts:118-126, vs state-before-config in Google/Microsoft), GitHub uses a JSON body + fetchWithRetry + no AbortSignal timeout + different failure predicate (!response.ok || data.error || !data.access_token) and a different catch-path message, and Slack keys success on data.ok with no timeout and uses config.redirectUri instead of deriving from req. Forcing them in would either change behavior or bloat the hook surface; mention them as possible future consumers at most.

6. No test exercises either callback handler today, so the existing suite will not catch regressions here; add a couple of handler-level tests (mock fetch, assert the exact postMessage payload JSON per branch — type/service/email/error fields byte-for-byte) before or with the refactor.

7. Keep the per-file state machinery (generate/consume, HKDF domain strings 'pulsarteam:oauth-state:google:v1' vs ':microsoft:v1', Microsoft's consumerFlow field) out of this change, as the proposal already says — it is the subject of the separate state-store-factory proposal, and googleOAuthState.test.ts depends on the Google exports staying put.

title: getGitHubCredentialsForAgent re-implements the scope-fallback chain with quietly different semantics
description: github.ts:149-177 walks agent→board scopes by hand (lines 154-167) to capture meta.login, then calls resolveAccessToken for the user fallback. The hand-rolled walk uses cache-only getOAuthToken while resolveAccessToken's equivalent steps use fetchOAuthTokenWithDbFallback (oauthTokens.ts:284) — so the same provider has two fallback chains with diverging cache/DB semantics: an agent-scoped token persisted by another replica is found by getGitHubAccessTokenForAgent but missed by getGitHubCredentialsForAgent's first loop (it then silently falls through to resolveAccessToken, which finds it — but reports login: null). This check-then-act split exists only because resolveAccessToken returns a bare string and drops the record meta.
sketch:
Add to oauthTokens.ts next to resolveAccessToken:

export async function resolveOAuthTokenRecord(provider, agentId, boardId): Promise<{ record: OAuthTokenRecord; scopeType: ScopeType } | null>

(same walk, returns the record instead of the string; resolveAccessToken can delegate to it). getGitHubCredentialsForAgent then becomes ~8 lines: const hit = await resolveOAuthTokenRecord('github', agentId, boardId); return hit ? { token: hit.record.accessToken, login: hit.scopeType === 'user' ? null : hit.record.meta?.login ?? null, provider: 'github' } : null; — the scopeType guard preserves today's login: null on user-scope fallback exactly. The 7 call sites (chat.ts, crud.ts, tasks.ts, tools.ts, terminal.ts, actionExecutor.ts) are untouched.
impact: ~30 lines → ~8 in github.ts; removes the second, semantically-drifted copy of the fallback chain; the agent/board DB-fallback inconsistency disappears as a side effect
guidance:
Three corrections to the sketch, one declared behavior delta to document:

1. SIGNATURE: resolveOAuthTokenRecord(provider, agentId, boardId) cannot absorb resolveAccessToken's walk as sketched, because the walk interleaves expiry-check + refreshFn + continue-on-failure per scope (oauthTokens.ts:290-299: refresh failure must CONTINUE to the next scope, which is impossible if the walk already returned a record). Amend to resolveOAuthTokenRecord(provider, agentId, boardId, refreshFn?) returning { record, scopeType, accessToken } | null where accessToken is the post-refresh token (=== record.accessToken when no refresh occurred). resolveAccessToken then becomes: const hit = await resolveOAuthTokenRecord(...); if (!hit) throw new Error(`Not connected to ${provider}. Please authenticate first.`); return hit.accessToken; — preserving the exact throw message and the refresh semantics for gmail/gdrive/onedrive/outlook (gmail.ts:87, gdrive.ts:88, onedrive.ts:88, outlook.ts:89 pass refreshFns). Keep the expiry-skip (expired + no refresh → continue) inside the walk; for github it is a no-op since github tokens are stored without expiresAt (github.ts:235-241).

2. BARREL + TEST MOCK: export resolveOAuthTokenRecord from the api/src/services/database.ts barrel (line 48 re-export list), AND add `resolveOAuthTokenRecord: async () => null` to the mock.module namedExports in api/src/services/__tests__/workflow-pipeline.test.ts (lines 19-131, see resolveAccessToken at line 129). The mock is an explicit namedExports list — a missing named import is an ESM link error that fails the whole test file, since github.ts is loaded transitively.

3. KEEP the sketch's scopeType === 'user' ? null : meta?.login guard — it is load-bearing. User-scoped records DO carry meta.login (stored unconditionally at github.ts:240), so dropping the guard would change the user-scope fallback path too.

DECLARED BEHAVIOR DELTA (accept, but note in the commit message): in the cold-cache/cross-replica window, an agent- or board-scoped token found via DB fallback now reports its meta.login instead of null. Observable effect is confined to the git credential username sent to runner-service (runnerExecutionProvider.ts:115/202 → agent_user.py:424-427/467, null → 'x-access-token' fallback); GitHub accepts both per runner-service's own comment, login is its stated preference, and the token is identical either way. Trivial non-issue: the old first loop required tok.accessToken truthy (github.ts:160) while the walk only checks record existence — only distinguishable for empty-string tokens, and every consumer gates on creds.token truthiness (runnerExecutionProvider.ts:83, chat.ts:112, tools.ts:477, terminal.ts:287).
